section.title: The Migration of Dan
section.sectionRationale: The second half of the first epilogue follows Micah's idols out of his house and into the life of a whole tribe. Dan, having failed to take the inheritance God assigned, sends spies who lodge with Micah, hear his Levite pronounce a blessing, and go on to scout the quiet town of Laish. A war party of six hundred returns, strips Micah of his images and his priest, slaughters the peaceful people of Laish, and founds a new city named Dan, where they set up the stolen idols. It is one movement that turns a household's private idolatry into a tribe's public shrine that will corrupt Israel for generations.
section.thesis: A tribe that will not receive the inheritance God gave instead seizes what it wants by force, carrying off another man's gods and priest, butchering an unsuspecting people, and enshrining idolatry at Dan, so that self-made religion, once begun in a single home, spreads to poison the whole nation, all because there is no king and no fear of the LORD.
section.frameNote: Six beats: the Danites seek land and lodge with Micah, verses 1 to 6; the spies report on Laish, verses 7 to 10; the armed company returns to Micah's house, verses 11 to 13; the seizure of the idols and the Levite, verses 14 to 21; Micah's protest overpowered, verses 22 to 26; and Laish destroyed, Dan founded in idolatry, verses 27 to 31.

units.label: The Danites seek land and lodge with Micah
units.note: the LORD is watching over your journey

verses.text: In those days there was no {{melek|king}} in Israel, and the tribe of the Danites was looking for territory to occupy. For up to that time they had not come into an {{nachalah|inheritance}} among the tribes of Israel.

So the Danites sent out five men from their clans, men of valor from Zorah and Eshtaol, to spy out the land and explore it. “Go and explore the land,” they told them. The men entered the hill country of Ephraim and came to the house of Micah, where they spent the night.
And while they were near Micah's house, they recognized the voice of the young Levite; so they went over and asked him, “Who brought you here? What are you doing in this place? Why are you here?”
“Micah has done this and that for me,” he replied, “and he has hired me to be his priest.”
Then they said to him, “Please inquire of God to determine whether we will have a successful journey.”
And the priest told them, “Go in peace. The LORD is watching over your journey.”

So the five men departed and came to Laish, where they saw that the people were living securely, like the Sidonians, quiet and unsuspecting. There was nothing lacking in the land and no oppressive ruler. And they were far away from the Sidonians and had no alliance with anyone.
When the men returned to Zorah and Eshtaol, their brothers asked them, “What did you find?”
They answered, “Come on, let us go up against them, for we have seen the land, and it is very good. Why would you fail to act? Do not hesitate to go there and take possession of the land!
When you enter, you will come to an unsuspecting people and a spacious land, for God has delivered it into your hand. It is a place where nothing on earth is lacking.”

units.label: The armed company returns to Micah's house
units.note: six hundred armed with weapons of war

verses.text: So six hundred Danites departed from Zorah and Eshtaol, armed with weapons of war.
They went up and camped at Kiriath-jearim in Judah. That is why the place west of Kiriath-jearim is called Mahaneh-dan to this day.
And from there they traveled to the hill country of Ephraim and came to Micah's house.

units.label: The seizure of the idols and the Levite
units.note: come with us and be a father and a priest to us

verses.text: Then the five men who had gone to spy out the land of Laish said to their brothers, “Did you know that one of these houses has an {{ephod|ephod}}, {{teraphim|household gods}}, a graven image, and a molten idol? Now think about what you should do.”
So they turned aside there and went to the home of the young Levite, the house of Micah, and greeted him.
The six hundred Danites stood at the entrance of the gate, armed with their weapons of war.

And the five men who had gone to spy out the land went inside and took the graven image, the ephod, the household idols, and the molten idol, while the priest stood at the entrance of the gate with the six hundred armed men.
When they entered Micah's house and took the graven image, the ephod, the household idols, and the molten idol, the priest said to them, “What are you doing?”

“Be quiet,” they told him. “Put your hand over your mouth and come with us and be a father and a priest to us. Is it better for you to be a priest for the house of one person or a priest for a tribe and family in Israel?”
So the priest was glad and took the ephod, the household idols, and the graven image, and went with the people.
Putting their small children, their livestock, and their possessions in front of them, they turned and departed.

units.label: Micah's protest overpowered
units.note: you took the gods I had made

verses.text: After they were some distance from Micah's house, the men in the houses near Micah's house mobilized and overtook the Danites.
When they called out after them, the Danites turned to face them and said to Micah, “What is the matter with you that you have called out such a company?”
He replied, “You took the gods I had made, and my priest, and went away. What else do I have? How can you say to me, ‘What is the matter with you?’”
The Danites said to him, “Do not raise your voice against us, or angry men will attack you, and you and your family will lose your lives.”
So the Danites went on their way, and Micah turned to go back home, because he saw that they were too strong for him.

units.label: Laish destroyed, Dan founded in idolatry
units.note: they set up for themselves Micah's graven image

verses.text: After they had taken Micah's idols and his priest, they went to Laish, to a quiet and unsuspecting people, and they struck them with their swords and burned down the city.
There was no one to deliver them, because the city was far from Sidon and had no alliance with anyone; it was in a valley near Beth-rehob. And the Danites rebuilt the city and lived there.
They named it Dan, after their forefather Dan, who was born to Israel, though the city was formerly named Laish.
The Danites set up idols for themselves, and Jonathan son of Gershom, the son of Moses, and his sons were priests for the tribe of Dan until the day of the captivity of the land.
So they set up for themselves Micah's {{pesel|graven image}}, and it was there the whole time the house of God was in Shiloh.

melek.gloss: King, ruler. The refrain in those days there was no king in Israel opens this chapter as it did the last, interpreting the violence and idolatry that follow: with no king to uphold God's law, a whole tribe does what is right in its own eyes. The cry points ahead to David and finally to Christ, the true King.

nachalah.gloss: Inheritance, allotted possession. God had assigned Dan a portion, Joshua 19:40-48, but they failed to take it, so here they seek land elsewhere. The word exposes the root of the chapter: a tribe that would not receive God's gift by faith grasps instead by force what God never gave them at Laish.

ephod.gloss: A priestly garment used at the sanctuary to seek God's guidance, Exodus 28. Micah's counterfeit ephod is carried off with his other idols to Dan, where it anchors a rival worship; the object meant for approaching the true God becomes a fixture of false religion.

teraphim.gloss: Household gods or idols kept for divination, later ranked with rebellion and witchcraft, 1 Samuel 15:23. The Danites covet Micah's teraphim along with his images, showing that their interest in worship is bound up with securing luck and success rather than obeying the LORD.

pesel.gloss: A carved or graven image, forbidden by the second commandment, Exodus 20:4. Micah's pesel, made from silver once dedicated to the LORD, ends the chapter enshrined at Dan while the true house of God stands at Shiloh, a lasting monument to worship gone wrong.

basic.context: The idols Micah made in chapter 17 now travel. The tribe of Dan had never taken the land God assigned them, so they go hunting for territory. Their spies lodge at Micah's house, recognize his hired Levite, and get from him a smooth blessing for their journey. They scout Laish, a peaceful, defenseless town, and report that it is ripe for the taking. Six hundred armed Danites march up, stop at Micah's house, and steal his carved image, ephod, household gods, and molten idol, then persuade his Levite to come and be priest to a whole tribe instead of one man. When Micah protests, they threaten his life and he backs down. They fall on quiet Laish, slaughter the people, burn the city, rebuild it as Dan, and set up the stolen idols there, founding a center of false worship that lasts for generations while God's true house stands at Shiloh.
basic.hermeneutics: This is description of covenant collapse, not prescription. The narrator reports the theft, the massacre, and the idolatry without a word of approval, and the opening refrain about having no king is his judgment on it all. The Danites use religious language, they inquire of God and speak of Him delivering the land, but their actions are godless: covetous, violent, and idolatrous. The chapter shows how private sin, left unchecked, becomes public and national, and it warns God's people not to dress up self-will in the language of faith.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter opens with the word melek, king: there was no king in Israel. Dan had failed to take its nachalah, its God-given inheritance, and so grasps land by force instead. The stolen objects, the ephod and the teraphim, the household idols, are the machinery of false worship, and the pesel, the carved image, ends the chapter enshrined at Dan. The words trace a straight line from a tribe's disobedience to a nation's idolatry.
basic.typology: Everything here is a dark contrast to Christ and His kingdom. The Danites take an inheritance by slaughter; Christ secures an inheritance for His people by giving His own life. They set up a false priest and stolen gods; He is the true Priest who leads us to the true God. The peaceful town destroyed without a deliverer makes us long for the true Deliverer, and the whole chapter's lawless violence cries out for the righteous King the refrain names.
basic.christ: The refrain there was no king in Israel presses hardest here, where the absence of a king means a tribe murders the innocent and enshrines idols. The story aches for a King who rules in righteousness, and that King is Jesus. Where Dan seized an inheritance by the sword, Christ wins an eternal inheritance for His people by His cross. Where Dan set up a counterfeit priest and false gods, Christ is the true and living way to God. The gospel shines against this dark backdrop: the King has come to end idolatry, to save rather than destroy, and to give His people the inheritance they could never seize for themselves.
basic.god: God's providence and God's justice both hover over the chapter. The Danites claim He is delivering the land to them, and in His mysterious sovereignty history moves as He permits, yet the narrator makes plain that their idolatry and violence are sin, not obedience. God had given Dan an inheritance they refused; their grasping at Laish is unbelief turned to force. And the closing note that all this stood while the house of God was at Shiloh reminds us that God had appointed a true place and way of worship the whole time, which they abandoned. He is the God who judges idolatry, in time the shrine at Dan and the tribe itself fall into captivity.
basic.crossReferences: Joshua 19 records the inheritance Dan failed to take, the root of their wandering. Exodus 20 forbids the carved images they enshrine. 1 Kings 12 shows Jeroboam later setting a golden calf at Dan, the idolatry begun here reaching its infamous height. And 1 Peter 1 speaks of the imperishable inheritance kept in heaven for God's people through Christ, the true possession Dan grasped after in vain.
basic.oneStory: Micah's private idolatry becomes a tribe's public shrine, and a peaceful people are slaughtered so that self-made religion can plant itself at Dan, all because there is no king. The story shows sin metastasizing through the nation and cries out for the righteous King and true Deliverer. That cry is answered in Jesus, who ends idolatry, saves the helpless, and gives His people an inheritance no one can seize or destroy.

context.literary: This chapter completes the first appendix of Judges, carrying Micah's idols from chapter 17 into the story of a tribe. It mirrors and darkens the earlier spy narratives of the conquest: scouts, a report of good land, a call to go up, but now the target is a peaceful Israelite-adjacent town and the prize is loot and idols rather than the promised inheritance. The refrain there was no king in Israel binds it to chapter 17 and to the whole epilogue.
context.historical: Dan had been allotted territory in the south near the Philistines but could not hold it, Joshua 19 and Judges 1:34, so this migration relocates the tribe to the far north. Laish, later Dan, sat near the headwaters of the Jordan, isolated and prosperous. A shrine there, with a Levitical priesthood tracing to Moses' own grandson, made Dan a rival worship center long before Jeroboam placed a golden calf there. The note about the captivity of the land points to a later deportation, dating the final form of the account.
context.canonical: The idolatry founded here becomes notorious in Israel's history; Dan and Bethel are the two shrines of the northern kingdom, 1 Kings 12:28-30, and Dan is remembered as a source of covenant unfaithfulness. The chapter thus explains a stain that runs deep into the monarchy and helps account for the exile. Against it stands the promised inheritance and true worship, fulfilled not in a stolen shrine but in Christ and the living temple of His people.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show how the private idolatry of one household spread to corrupt a whole tribe and, through Dan, the nation, and to expose the violence and unbelief of a people who would not receive God's gift or obey His word, so that the reader longs for the righteous King the refrain names.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the theft, the massacre of Laish, and the founding of idolatry at Dan; it commends none of it. What endures is the warning that unchecked sin spreads, that religious language can mask godless deeds, and that God's people need the true King and true worship this counterfeit cannot supply.

debates.question: Does the narrator approve the Danites' conquest of Laish, since the priest and the spies speak of God's favor?

views.label: Reported speech, not the narrator's verdict
views.case: The claims that the LORD watches over the journey and that God has delivered the land are the words of a hired idolatrous priest and covetous spies; the narrator frames the whole account with there was no king in Israel and lets the violence and idolatry condemn themselves.

views.label: A twisted echo of the real conquest
views.case: The language deliberately recalls the God-commanded conquest of Canaan, but here it is misapplied to an unprovoked attack on a peaceful people for the sake of plunder and idols, showing how far Israel has fallen from genuine obedience.

debates.bearing: Either way the reader is not to bless the migration but to see covenant collapse, and to long for the righteous rule the refrain calls for.

debates.question: Why does the chapter stress that Dan failed to take its inheritance?

views.label: Unbelief at the root of the idolatry
views.case: God had given Dan a portion, Joshua 19, which they would not take by faith; their grasping at Laish and their embrace of idols flow from the same failure to trust and obey the LORD.

views.label: A contrast with God's provision
views.case: The emphasis exposes the folly of seizing by force what God did not give while forsaking what He did; it sets human grasping against divine gift, a contrast fulfilled in the sure inheritance Christ secures.

debates.bearing: Both readings warn against unbelief that abandons God's gift and takes matters, and worship, into its own hands, and both point ahead to the inheritance kept for God's people in Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key words trace the descent from disobedience to national idolatry. It opens, as chapter 17 did, with melek, king: there was no king in Israel. That absence is not merely political; it means a tribe can murder a peaceful town and enshrine stolen gods with no one to hold it to God's law. Behind the whole migration lies nachalah, inheritance: Dan had been given a portion by God, Joshua 19:40-48, and would not take it, so they grasp instead at Laish. The objects they covet are the tools of false worship, the ephod, the priestly garment meant for the sanctuary, and the teraphim, household idols bound up with divination and later named alongside witchcraft, 1 Samuel 15:23. At the end stands the pesel, the carved image forbidden by the second commandment, Exodus 20:4, set up at Dan while the true house of God remains at Shiloh. King refused, inheritance forsaken, idols enshrined: the vocabulary itself narrates a people who have turned from the LORD to gods of their own making, and it leaves us longing for the King who will put worship right.

typology.type: The inheritance seized by violence at Laish
typology.fulfillment: The imperishable inheritance Christ secures for His people not by the sword but by His own blood, 1 Peter 1:3-4 and Ephesians 1:11-14.

typology.type: The counterfeit priest and stolen gods enshrined at Dan
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true Priest and the true and living way, through whom alone we come to the living God, Hebrews 10:19-22.

typology.type: The refrain there was no king in Israel over a scene of bloodshed
typology.fulfillment: Christ the righteous King, who rules in justice, saves rather than destroys, and ends idolatry, Isaiah 9:6-7 and Revelation 11:15.

study.christ: Nowhere does the cry there was no king in Israel land harder than here, where the lack of a king means an innocent town is slaughtered and idols are enshrined at Dan. The whole chapter aches for a King who rules in righteousness, and that King is Jesus. The Danites take an inheritance by the sword, murdering the defenseless; Christ wins an everlasting inheritance for His people by laying down His own life, and He comes not to destroy the helpless but to save them. The Danites set up a bought priest and stolen gods; Christ is the true Priest who brings us to the true and living God and ends every counterfeit. Against the darkness of this story the gospel shines all the brighter: the King has come, idolatry is overthrown, and the inheritance Dan grasped after in vain is given freely to all who are His.
study.god: God's sovereignty and His justice both stand over the chapter. The spies and the priest speak glibly of the LORD watching their journey and God delivering the land, and in His overruling providence history unfolds as He permits; yet the narrator never lets us mistake this for obedience. God had given Dan an inheritance they refused, so their violence at Laish is unbelief turned to force, not faith. The closing reminder that all of this stood while the house of God was at Shiloh underscores that God had appointed a true place and way of worship the entire time, which the tribe abandoned for a shrine of stolen idols. He is the God who sees, who judges idolatry in His time, the note about captivity foreshadows Dan's fall, and who preserves true worship even when His people forsake it.

crossReferences.note: The inheritance allotted to Dan, which they failed to take, the background to their migration north.
The second commandment against carved images, the very idols the Danites enshrine at Dan.
Jeroboam sets a golden calf at Dan; the idolatry begun in Judges 18 reaches its infamous height and leads Israel into sin.
The imperishable inheritance kept in heaven for God's people through Christ, the true possession Dan grasped after in vain.

study.oneStory: Micah's household idols become a tribe's public shrine as Dan forsakes the inheritance God gave, slaughters peaceful Laish, and enshrines stolen gods, all because there is no king. The story shows sin spreading from one home to poison a nation, and it leaves us longing for the righteous King and true Deliverer it lacks. That longing is met in Jesus, who ends idolatry, rescues the helpless, and gives His people an inheritance no violence can win and no enemy can destroy, so that even this bleak chapter bends toward the hope of the gospel.

quiz.q: Why was the tribe of Dan looking for new territory?

options.text: They had not taken the inheritance God had already assigned them.
options.note: Verse 1 notes they had not come into an inheritance, the root failure behind the migration.

options.text: Their original land had become too crowded to support them.
options.note: They did need land, but the text stresses they had failed to possess the inheritance God gave, not mere overcrowding.

options.text: God had commanded them to relocate to the far north.
options.note: No such command is given; they act on their own, not by God's direction.

options.text: They were driven out by a foreign invasion.
options.note: The chapter describes their own grasping migration, not an expulsion by invaders.

quiz.q: How did the Danites treat Micah and his household worship?

options.text: They stole his idols and his priest and threatened his life when he protested.
options.note: Verses 17-26 show the theft and the threat that silenced Micah.

options.text: They persuaded his Levite to leave with them for a bigger role.
options.note: True of the priest, but they also seized the images by force and threatened Micah, which this misses.

options.text: They paid Micah a fair price for his shrine and its objects.
options.note: No payment is made; they take everything by intimidation.

options.text: They destroyed his shrine and rebuked him for idolatry.
options.note: They did not rebuke idolatry; they carried the idols off to practice the same false worship.

quiz.q: What happened to the people of Laish?

options.text: The Danites struck this quiet, unsuspecting people with the sword and burned their city.
options.note: Verses 27-28 describe the slaughter of a peaceful, defenseless town.

options.text: They were conquered after a hard-fought battle.
options.note: There was no real battle; the people were quiet and unsuspecting, with no one to deliver them.

options.text: They surrendered and were allowed to live under Danite rule.
options.note: The text says the Danites struck them and burned the city, not that they were spared.

options.text: They drove off the Danite attack.
options.note: The opposite; there was no one to deliver them and the city was destroyed.

quiz.q: What did the Danites establish at their new city of Dan?

options.text: They set up Micah's carved image and a priesthood, founding a center of idolatry that lasted for generations.
options.note: Verses 30-31 record the enshrined idol and priesthood that endured while God's house was at Shiloh.

options.text: They set up a shrine but soon returned to worship at Shiloh.
options.note: The idols were set up and remained; there is no return to Shiloh, which stands in pointed contrast.

options.text: They built an altar to the LORD according to the law of Moses.
options.note: They used a priest descended from Moses, but the worship was idolatrous, not the law's appointed worship.

options.text: They abandoned worship altogether.
options.note: They did not abandon worship; they founded a lasting center of false worship at Dan.

quiz.q: How should we read the Danites' talk of God favoring their journey?

options.text: As the self-serving words of covetous men and a hired idolatrous priest, which the narrator does not endorse.
options.note: The refrain about no king frames the account as covenant collapse, not divine approval.

options.text: As sincere but mistaken piety that God gently tolerated.
options.note: There is religious language, but the chapter exposes it as a mask over violence and idolatry, not tolerated piety.

options.text: As proof that God commanded and blessed the attack on Laish.
options.note: The narrator gives no divine command; the God-talk is the characters' own, and the deeds are condemned.

options.text: As words that make no difference to how we judge the story.
options.note: They matter as a warning: godless deeds are here wrapped in godly language, which the reader must discern.

quiz.q: How does this dark chapter point us toward Christ?

options.text: Its lawlessness and stolen inheritance make us long for the righteous King who saves and gives a sure inheritance.
options.note: The refrain cries for a king; Christ rules in justice and secures an eternal inheritance, 1 Peter 1:3-4.

options.text: It teaches that God's people may take what they want if they call it His will.
options.note: The chapter warns against exactly this; it does not commend seizing by force under religious cover.

options.text: It shows that idolatry is harmless as long as a Levite is involved.
options.note: The Levite's presence only deepens the guilt; the idolatry is condemned, not excused.

options.text: It has nothing to do with Christ or the gospel.
options.note: The cry for a king and the failed inheritance point directly to Christ, the King and the true inheritance.

questions: Dan would not receive the inheritance God had given, then grasped by force what He never gave them. Where are you tempted to seize on your own terms what you will not wait to receive from God?
The spies and the priest spoke easily of God's favor while pursuing plunder and idols. How can you learn to test religious language, in yourself and others, by what God has actually said?
Micah's private idols became a tribe's public shrine. How does this warn you to deal with hidden sin before it spreads to those around you?
The people of Laish were struck down with no one to deliver them. How does this drive your longing for the true Deliverer, and your compassion for the defenseless around you?

The refrain there was no king in Israel hangs over a scene of violence and idolatry. What does it stir in you to know that the righteous King has come in Jesus, and what would it mean to live under His rule this week?

translationNotes.note: Mahaneh-dan, the place name in verse 12, means camp of Dan, as the BSB footnote explains; the narrator marks the trail of the migration with names that lasted to his own day.
The city is renamed Dan after the tribe's forefather; one em dash in the source here, born to Israel to though, has been rendered as a clean break with a comma, as throughout.

The BSB reads Jonathan son of Gershom, the son of Moses, with a footnote that other manuscripts read the son of Manasseh; an ancient scribal tradition softened the shock that Moses' own grandson served an idol shrine.
The closing line contrasts Micah's graven image set up at Dan with the house of God at Shiloh, underscoring that a true place of worship stood the whole time this false one flourished.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 18:1-31. One em dash in the source at verse 29, Israel to though, converted to a comma; none anywhere in the file. Sermon set to none; no verified expository sermon on Judges 18 from the trusted cluster confirmed this session.
